detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do urban stress factors change as you travel from Esil district to Sararka district?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is clearly geographical and linked to the MultipleâNuclei model, enabling testable hypotheses.
Precise locational context with named districts, coordinates, and a wellâjustified transect of 19 sites.
Highâquality maps with appropriate symbology, legends, north arrows and scale bars.
weaknesses: Minor cartographic blemishes (e.g. missing scale bar on Kazakhstan map).
Some theoretical citations come from nonâpeerâreviewed sources.
Could annotate maps further (road names/landmarks) to enhance spatial orientation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the topâband descriptors: a clearly formulated geographical question with theoretical links (3/3), precise location allowing feasible primary data collection (3/3), and clear mapping conventions (3/3). Minor cartographic and citation improvements do not detract from full achievement of the criterion.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods (systematic sampling, noise app, landâuse survey, bipolar survey) are comprehensively described and theoretically aligned.
Justification of each method in relation to hypotheses demonstrates clear relevance.
weaknesses: Data collection yielded limited reliability due to short 5-minute noise measurements and minimal replication.
Inconsistency in reported number of sites (19 vs. 20) reduces methodological transparency.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Methods are well described and linked (meets band 3), but implementation issues (limited replication, site count inconsistency) mean data collection only reaches âcorrectly used but limitedâ (band 2), warranting an overall score of 2/3.

strengths: All primary data are directly relevant to the hypotheses (noise, park counts, urban quality scores).
A good variety of presentation techniquesâgraphs, tables, GIS mapsâare appropriate to the data type.
Cartographic and graph conventions are largely respected (titles, axes, legends).
weaknesses: Occasional typographical and unitâlabelling errors (e.g. inconsistent dB notation).
Some axes or figure titles are missing, and bibliography style is inconsistent.
Presentation could be more polished (axis labels, figure numbering).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data relevance and sufficiency consistently meet the 3-4 band (marks 3â4), presentation techniques are appropriate and mostly clear (band 4), and conventions are followed with occasional errors (band 3). Overall this merits the top of the 3-4 band, a score of 4/6.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive statistics and Spearmanâs rank correlation, demonstrating sound technique.
Insightful interpretation of anomalies (e.g. deviating noise levels in Keruen City).
Discussion links trends to the Multiple-Nuclei model and research question.
weaknesses: No confidence intervals or error bars in graphs, limiting statistical completeness.
Interpretation of Hypotheses 2 and 3 lacks depth and quantitative support.
P-value reported without threshold or test statistic details, reducing clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis shows both descriptive and inferential techniques (meeting band 3â4), but gaps in statistical completeness and uneven interpretation of hypotheses place it at the lower end of that band, a score of 3/8.

strengths: A conclusion is provided and draws on noise analysis.
There is at least some direct linkage to the research question.
weaknesses: Hypotheses 2 and 3 are only briefly referenced, leaving parts of the study unaddressed.
Support from the analysis is partial rather than comprehensive.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion exists and is partially supported by the analysis, but it does not fully synthesise all findings or hypotheses, fitting the descriptor for band 1/2.

strengths: Evaluation outlines key strengths and weaknesses of the methods and data.
Improvement suggestions (e.g. longer noise monitoring, larger sample) are relevant and linked to data quality.
weaknesses: Discussion of improvements lacks detail on implementation timeline and resource implications.
Evaluation could deepen the link between each improvement and its expected impact.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation provides an appropriate outline of strengths/weaknesses and relevant improvement suggestions, matching the middle descriptor for band 2/3, thus earning 2/3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Multiple scaled maps show Kazakhstan, the city, district boundaries and the exact transect with sampling points, all containing north arrows, legends and scale bars.  Although minor cartographic blemishes exist, mapping conventions are largely respected and the spatial representation is clear and purposeful, justifying a top-band award.

The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, firmly embedded in the Urban Environments option, and explicitly lends itself to measurable hypotheses on noise, green space and housing quality.  The student ties it to the Multiple-Nuclei model, demonstrating a theoretical anchor that allows testable expectations.  This alignment with theory and the presence of three hypotheses satisfies the highest descriptor for this strand.

The student employs a good variety of presentation techniquesâgraphs, annotated photos, tables and GIS-derived mapsâappropriate to the data type.  Layout and labelling are generally clear; while not the âmost effectiveâ in every case, they comfortably surpass mere appropriateness, meriting the top of the 3-4 band.

Techniques were implemented correctly (19 evenly spaced sites, 5-minute noise readings, field surveys) and yielded a usable data set.  However, short measurement duration and limited replication reduce reliability, so the evidence reaches the âcorrectly used but limitedâ rather than âhigh-qualityâ standard, fitting band 2.

Descriptive statistics are combined with Spearmanâs rank correlation, an appropriate inferential test for ordinal data.  Confidence levels are not calculated, so the analysis is not fully comprehensive, but both descriptive and statistical techniques are present and largely accurate, matching the 3-4 descriptor.

The student links noise trends to land use and the Multiple-Nuclei model and comments on anomalies; however, interpretation of green-space and housing data is thinner and theoretical connections are partial.  This pattern of solid but not exhaustive linkage fits comfortably within the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Specific districts (Esil and Sararka) are named, coordinates for Astana are provided and a 10 km transect with 19 sites is mapped, making primary data collection clearly feasible.  The contrasts between the two districts are described, demonstrating precise locational knowledge that fulfils the top-band expectations.

The evaluation table outlines two strengths and two limitations with brief explanationsâmore than a superficial list but short of a comprehensive discussion.  This corresponds to an âappropriate outline of strengths/weaknessesâ, fitting band 2.

Noise, park counts and urban quality scores are all directly related to the hypotheses and generally sufficient for analysis, though depth is uneven (e.g., green-space data are simplistic).  This aligns with âmost data relevant allowing partial analysis,â warranting the upper mark of the 3-4 band.

A conclusion is provided but focuses mainly on noise levels, leaving the other two hypotheses only briefly referenced.  Support from analysis is partial and selective, so the work meets the descriptor for âpartially supported conclusionâ (band 1).

All three hypotheses are discussed, but analysis of Hypotheses 2 and 3 lacks depth and supporting calculations (e.g., only descriptive comments).  There are evidence gaps, yet a reasonable level of analysis is present, matching âanalysis with some gapsâ at 3 marks in the 3-4 band.

Methods (systematic sampling, noise app, land-use/height survey, bipolar survey) are fully described with justification and linked to each hypothesis and to the Multiple-Nuclei model.  The explanation is sufficiently detailed and theoretically aligned to meet the highest descriptor for this strand.

Most maps include key cartographic elements, and graphs contain titles and axes.  Nevertheless, typographical errors, inconsistent units (dB commas) and occasional unlabeled axes show âoccasional errorsâ, placing the work solidly in the 3-4 band but at the lower of the two marks.

Suggested improvements (synchronised timing, larger sample, longer noise monitoring) are relevant and their potential effect is briefly indicated, yet detail on implementation and impact is limited.  The work therefore aligns with the middle descriptorâârelevant improvement suggestions outlinedââearning 2 marks.

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 2 is clearly stated and directly tied to urban stress theory, supporting a testable prediction regarding greenâspace distribution.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The text for Hypothesis 3 contains typographical errors (e.g., âlolr-qualityâ). Proofread to ensure clarity and professionalism.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly geographical, focusing on urban stress gradients between districts, and is strongly linked to the Multiple-Nuclei model to generate measurable hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The table of contents is densely formatted and lacks clear hierarchy; reformat with consistent indentation and page references to improve navigation.
The Multiple-Nuclei model background is well explained, but consider citing a peer-reviewed source rather than a commercial website to strengthen academic rigour.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 1.1 effectively illustrates the model, but adding a brief annotation on how each nucleus relates to your hypotheses would clarify its relevance.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The geographical context description is detailed, including coordinates and landscape setting, which provides strong locational specificity for primary data collection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The map of Kazakhstan (Fig 1.2) lacks a scale bar; including one and verifying projection details would improve cartographic convention adherence.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The city map (Fig 1.3) clearly delineates districts with appropriate symbology and legend placement, adhering well to mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Your transect justification text ties landâuse contrasts to socio-economic context, demonstrating precise locational feasibility for primary data collection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 1.4 presents district boundaries effectively, but consider labeling major roads or landmarks to enhance spatial orientation for readers.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 1 is sharply phrased and linked explicitly to your theoretical model, enabling clear operationalization of noise measurements.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: There is an inconsistency in the number of sites (mention of 20 sites vs. 19 elsewhere). Clarify this discrepancy to ensure methodological transparency.
Noise measurements were taken for only five minutes without replication; consider longer durations and multiple readings to increase data reliability.
Figures 2.2 and 2.3 document fieldwork but could be consolidated or annotated with key variables to reduce redundancy and focus on data content.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Land Use survey is well linked to Hypothesis 2, demonstrating method relevance to your research question.
The Land Use and Building Height survey table (Fig 2.4) lacks clear axis labels and units; include these for consistent presentation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The bipolar survey method is described in depth, justifying its use for assessing housing quality and urban stress factors.
The bipolar survey table lacks figure numbering and clear column headings; adding these would improve clarity and adherence to conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The method name is misspelled as âSpiersmanâs rankâ; correct to âSpearmanâs rank correlationâ for methodological accuracy.
The methodology description details systematic sampling and site selection clearly, demonstrating strong alignment with the research questions.
Inclusion of the Spearmanâs Rank formula is appropriate; consider defining each parameter within the narrative to support reader comprehension.
Data analysis of Hypothesis 1 presents trends clearly, but adding confidence intervals or error bars on graphs would strengthen statistical interpretation.
Figure 3.1 lacks a descriptive title and axis labels; including these would improve data communication and adherence to presentation conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Analysis of anomalies (e.g., Keruen City) links local land-use factors to deviations, demonstrating insightful interpretation of patterns.
Reporting a p-value of 0.24 without specifying the significance threshold or test statistic reduces interpretive clarity; include Î± and critical values.
The null hypothesis discussion omits mention of critical values or degrees of freedom; include these to complete inferential testing transparency.
Second hypothesis analysis introduces new trends inconsistent with the stated hypothesis; ensure discussion matches the original research question.
Acknowledging irregular patterns of park distribution demonstrates sound data interpretation, linking local land-use decisions to hypothesis 2 outcomes.
Analysis of urban quality indicators for Hypothesis 3 effectively discusses variability, but consider integrating quantitative comparison for completeness.
The conclusion is truncated and does not address Hypotheses 2 and 3 fully; expand to summarise all findings and theoretical implications.
The concluding section on page 18 restates noise results but omits clear closure for green space and housing quality hypotheses; integrate these elements.
Strengths table effectively lists diverse methods, but could link each strength more directly to data outcomes for deeper evaluation.
The limitations table outlines relevant improvements, but adding timelines or resources needed would make suggestions more actionable.
Bibliography entries show inconsistency in style (e.g., italics usage); apply a consistent referencing format throughout (e.g., APA).
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
